title: Sourcegraph access tokens should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Sourcegraph access token detected in source code. Sourcegraph access tokens (sgp_ prefix) provide API authentication to Sourcegraph code intelligence platform, enabling comprehensive code search, navigation, and analysis operations. These tokens grant significant permissions including: searching and browsing code across all accessible repositories, accessing code intelligence features (go-to-definition, find-references, symbol search), managing and executing code searches with advanced filters, accessing repository metadata and commit history, configuring and managing code monitors and alerts, accessing batch changes and campaign management features, managing saved searches and search contexts, accessing code insights and analytics dashboards, executing GraphQL API queries for automation, managing user settings and preferences, accessing organization-wide code intelligence data, configuring and managing search notebooks, accessing browser extension and IDE integration features, managing code review and collaboration workflows, and accessing audit logs and usage analytics. Exposing Sourcegraph access tokens enables attackers to: access entire codebase and proprietary source code across all repositories, search for sensitive information including credentials and API keys embedded in code, analyze code patterns to identify security vulnerabilities, access commit history and developer activity patterns, extract intellectual property and confidential business logic, access organization-wide code metrics and analytics, manipulate code monitors creating false alerts or hiding critical issues, access batch changes potentially enabling widespread malicious code modifications, consume API rate limits disrupting legitimate code search operations, access code ownership and responsibility information for social engineering, enumerate repository structure and access patterns, access integration credentials for version control systems, exfiltrate code intelligence data for competitive advantage, and use the token to pivot to other systems integrated with Sourcegraph. This is a critical security vulnerability requiring immediate remediation. Take immediate action: revoke the exposed access token immediately from Sourcegraph user settings (User menu → Settings → Access tokens), generate a new access token with minimal necessary scopes and permissions, update all applications, CLI tools, IDE integrations, and automation scripts with the new token, review Sourcegraph audit logs for unauthorized access or suspicious search patterns, check which repositories and code the exposed token had access to, verify no malicious code monitors or batch changes were created, review all recent search queries and API usage for data exfiltration attempts, audit browser extension and IDE plugin configurations, implement principle of least privilege for token permissions (limit repository access and feature scopes), store Sourcegraph tokens in secure secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GitHub Secrets), use environment variables with restricted access for CLI and automation, never commit Sourcegraph tokens to version control or configuration files, implement secret scanning in git hooks and CI/CD pipelines, use separate tokens for different environments and purposes to limit blast radius, rotate Sourcegraph tokens regularly (every 90 days recommended), enable SSO and enforce MFA for Sourcegraph accounts, monitor API usage for anomalies and unusual search patterns, implement IP whitelisting for API access when possible, document all tokens in a secure credential inventory with purpose and scope, educate development teams about proper token handling and code intelligence platform security, integrate Sourcegraph token detection in pre-commit hooks and security scanning, and regularly audit token permissions and access patterns for security compliance.
